proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large firms employ a set of identifiable 'tolerance mechanisms'—analogous to immunological tolerance—that selectively permit strategically aligned entrepreneurial marketing while suppressing misaligned activities, and that the effectiveness of these mechanisms is positively associated with both innovation output and strategic coherence.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods design. First, we will conduct qualitative, multiple-case studies in 4-6 large, established firms known for entrepreneurial activity, using semi-structured interviews and archival data to inductively identify candidate tolerance mechanisms (e.g., sandbox budgets, innovation review boards, cultural narratives). Second, we will operationalize these mechanisms into a survey instrument and test our hypotheses using structural equation modeling (SEM) on a large-scale sample of senior managers and division heads from Fortune 1000 firms, controlling for firm size, industry, and performance.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, testable framework—the Corporate Immune System—that integrates literatures on corporate entrepreneurship, marketing, and governance. It will provide managers with a diagnostic tool to audit and design their firm's tolerance mechanisms. Theoretically, it advances understanding of the micro-processes that govern the interface between entrepreneurial initiative and corporate control.
proposals.novelty: While analogies like 'ambidexterity' exist, applying the precise biological concept of 'immunological tolerance'—with its focus on active suppression mechanisms—to the governance of entrepreneurial marketing is novel. It moves beyond describing the tension to specifying the systemic solutions firms employ, offering a fresh, mechanism-based perspective.

proposals.title: Internal Venturing as a Substitute for Networks: A Study of Absorptive Capacity and Capability Reconfiguration in Insular Firms
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in strategic entrepreneurship and international business (pre-2026) emphasizes the necessity of value network collaboration, alliances, and open innovation for building absorptive capacity and reconfiguring capabilities. This assumes external ties are indispensable for accessing and integrating new knowledge. However, this overlooks a potential class of firms that may successfully develop these capacities through intense internal processes, challenging a core tenet of network theory and resource-based views.
proposals.hypothesis: Firms with strong, deliberate internal venturing mechanisms and corporate entrepreneurship processes can develop absorptive capacity and achieve strategic capability reconfiguration without relying on significant external network collaboration, and these 'insular' firms can achieve competitive performance parity with their networked counterparts.
proposals.method: The study employs a mixed-methods, longitudinal design. First, a quantitative analysis of a panel dataset (e.g., COMPUSTAT matched with patent data) will use fixed-effects regression and propensity score matching to identify firms with high innovation output but low alliance/network intensity (measured by SDC Platinum data). Second, a qualitative multiple-case study of 4-6 matched pairs (insular vs. networked firms) will be conducted. Data collection includes archival analysis and executive interviews to trace the internal processes—such as internal corporate venturing, cross-divisional knowledge tournaments, and structured internal market mechanisms—used for capability building.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute to corporate entrepreneurship literature by theorizing an 'internal substitution' mechanism for network benefits. It will challenge the deterministic view in international business and alliance literature that external collaboration is a necessary condition for capability renewal, offering a more nuanced contingency theory of when and how firms can go it alone.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly challenging the assumed necessity of networks for absorptive capacity, a cornerstone of open innovation and alliance research, by systematically investigating firms that succeed through insular, internally-focused entrepreneurial processes.

proposals.title: Capability Orchestration in Turbulence: A Cross-Industry Analysis of Operating and Dynamic Capabilities
proposals.motivation: The dynamic capabilities view is central to strategic entrepreneurship, yet its interplay with operational efficiency remains underexplored across varied contexts. Pre-2026 research, including Zahra's work on corporate entrepreneurship, highlights a gap in understanding how this interaction drives firm adaptation and performance in different industry environments, from stable to hyper-competitive. This limits prescriptive value for entrepreneurs and managers seeking to configure capabilities effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of the interaction between operating and dynamic capabilities in driving firm performance is contingent on the industry's competitive dynamism and technological intensity, with dynamic capabilities playing a more pronounced role in high-velocity environments.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, cross-sectional design. First, a large-scale survey of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) across manufacturing, technology, and service sectors will measure constructs using validated scales for operational capabilities, dynamic capabilities (sensing, seizing, reconfiguring), and perceived environmental dynamism. Second, we will use hierarchical regression analysis and multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the moderating role of industry context. Qualitative case studies of entrepreneurial firms in contrasting sectors will provide nuanced insights into capability orchestration mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, context-contingent model of capability interaction, extending Zahra's integrative work on governance and entrepreneurship. It will offer empirical evidence to guide entrepreneurs in prioritizing capability development based on their industry's competitive landscape, bridging strategic management and entrepreneurship literatures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often treats capabilities as universally beneficial, this research explicitly tests their contingent value, moving beyond single-industry studies to provide a comparative framework that explains performance heterogeneity across sectors.

proposals.title: Beyond Smart Money: Entrepreneurs' Strategic Use of Equity Crowdfunding for Passive Capital and Governance Avoidance
proposals.motivation: Prior research on equity crowdfunding, including Zahra's own agenda, predominantly assumes entrepreneurs seek 'smart money'—investors who provide governance and strategic guidance. This overlooks a strategic alternative: entrepreneurs may deliberately use the dispersed, 'thinly-sliced' equity model of crowdfunding to secure capital while minimizing active investor oversight. This phenomenon challenges core assumptions in corporate entrepreneurship and venture finance about the entrepreneur-investor interface.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant segment of entrepreneurs pursuing equity crowdfunding are strategically motivated by a desire for passive capital and actively seek to resist or minimize formal investor governance, viewing the crowd's fragmentation as a feature, not a bug, for maintaining control.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method design. First, a quantitative survey of equity crowdfunding campaign founders (primarily from platforms like Crowdcube and Seedrs) will measure governance preferences, perceived investor activism, and control motives, using structural equation modeling to test relationships. Second, qualitative, in-depth interviews with a purposive sample of founders who successfully raised funds will explore the strategic narratives and tactics used to manage or circumvent crowd governance, analyzing transcripts through thematic coding.
proposals.contribution: This study will expand the theoretical understanding of entrepreneurial finance by introducing 'passive capital seeking' as a distinct strategic motive. It will refine models of the entrepreneur-investor interface in digital contexts and offer practical insights for platforms and policymakers on the governance trade-offs inherent in crowd-based equity.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges the entrenched 'smart money' paradigm in crowdfunding research and connects the literatures on entrepreneurial control preferences and the unique governance properties of fragmented equity ownership, a link not yet systematically explored.

proposals.title: Beyond the Dual Deficit: A Governance-Based Model of Marketing Excellence in Resource-Constrained SMEs
proposals.motivation: SMEs are often characterized as suffering from a dual deficit: a lack of sophisticated marketing skills and a reliance on simplistic, informal governance. This view overlooks how high-performing SMEs overcome constraints. Grounded in pre-2026 literature on corporate entrepreneurship and governance (e.g., Zahra's work on entrepreneurial governance and resource orchestration), this study addresses a critical gap by investigating how SMEs that excel at marketing actually govern this function to build a sustainable competitive advantage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that marketing excellence in SMEs is not a function of formal or informal governance alone, but is driven by a strategic, resourceful, and dynamic configuration of both—where formal contracts and informal relational norms complement each other to create a difficult-to-imitate marketing capability that evolves with firm growth and market changes.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods design. First, a qualitative multiple-case study of 15-20 purposefully selected high-marketing-performing SMEs will be conducted, using semi-structured interviews and archival data to map governance configurations. Second, findings will inform a large-scale survey to test the proposed model. Analysis will utilize structural equation modeling (SEM) to examine the relationships between governance constructs (formal/informal mix), dynamic marketing capabilities, and firm performance, controlling for firm size, age, and industry.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute to the fields of entrepreneurship and marketing by: (1) developing a mid-range theory of governance for entrepreneurial marketing, challenging the simplistic dual-deficit assumption; (2) providing actionable insights for SME managers on how to deliberately design governance systems to foster marketing excellence; and (3) extending corporate entrepreneurship theory by explicating the governance mechanisms that underpin strategic entrepreneurial capabilities within smaller firms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing governance theory (transaction cost economics & relational view) with the resource-based view in an SME context to conceptualize marketing excellence as a governance outcome, moving beyond descriptive studies of SME marketing practices to explain *how* superior marketing is systematically governed and sustained.

proposals.title: Micro-Governance in Minimalist Crowdfunding: The Emergence of Formal and Informal Control in Very Small Equity Campaigns
proposals.motivation: Prior research on equity crowdfunding (ECF) governance, including Zahra's integrative model, focuses on campaigns with many investors, where formal mechanisms are presumed necessary. However, the majority of ECF campaigns are small, raising modest sums from a handful of micro-investors. It is unknown if or how governance structures emerge in these minimalist settings, creating a critical gap in understanding post-funding dynamics for the most common entrepreneurial ventures using ECF.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in very small ECF rounds, formal governance is largely absent, but distinct, relationship-based informal governance mechanisms emerge, which are significantly influenced by the entrepreneur's prior experience and the campaign's communication narrative.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, multi-platform design. First, we will scrape and analyze archival data from platforms like Seedrs and Crowdcube (pre-2026) for all campaigns with ≤10 investors and ≤£50k raised, using text-mining algorithms (e.g., Latent Dirichlet Allocation) on campaign updates and forum posts to map communication themes. Second, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 30-40 entrepreneur-investor dyads from these campaigns, using thematic analysis to identify informal monitoring and advising routines. Third, we will use a configurational approach (fsQCA) to test which combinations of factors (e.g., investor homogeneity, communication frequency, entrepreneur background) lead to effective informal governance outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend Zahra's governance model 'downward' to the understudied domain of micro-ECF, theorizing the 'small business' logic of informal investor-entrepreneur interfaces. It will provide actionable insights for entrepreneurs on managing micro-investor relationships and for platforms on designing lightweight support tools for small campaigns.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant governance paradigm by investigating settings where formal mechanisms are scaled away, challenging the assumption that investor plurality is a prerequisite for governance and exploring how entrepreneurial agency fills the void.

proposals.title: Beyond the Universal: A Configurational Approach to Studying the Entrepreneurial Interface in Corporate and International Contexts
proposals.motivation: The persistent search for a single, universal definition of entrepreneurship has constrained theoretical development and empirical research, particularly at critical interfaces such as corporate entrepreneurship and international business. Pre-2026 literature, including Zahra's own work, highlights the multifaceted nature of entrepreneurship, yet studies often rely on narrow, inconsistent operationalizations. This creates a fragmented understanding of how entrepreneurial activities manifest and create value across different organizational and national contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adopting a configurational, context-dependent view of entrepreneurship—where it is defined by combinations of core dimensions (e.g., innovation, risk-taking, proactiveness) that vary in salience—will provide a more robust and nuanced framework for explaining performance outcomes at the corporate-international interface.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, multi-study design. First, a qualitative meta-synthesis of pre-2026 literature will identify key definitional dimensions and contextual contingencies. Second, we will use fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) on a longitudinal dataset of multinational corporations to model how different configurations of entrepreneurial dimensions (treating them as separate causal conditions) combine with contextual factors (e.g., institutional distance, market dynamism) to explain variance in corporate venturing success and international adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, pluralistic framework for studying entrepreneurship that moves beyond definitional debates. It will offer concrete, configurational models that scholars and managers can use to diagnose and design entrepreneurial initiatives tailored to specific corporate and international settings, thereby bridging the entrepreneurship, international business, and strategy literatures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly rejecting the quest for a universal definition as a prerequisite for research and instead operationalizing entrepreneurship as a set of combinable, context-contingent dimensions, analyzed through fsQCA—a method suited to capturing causal complexity at the interface of different business domains.

proposals.title: Thematic Convergence in Entrepreneurial Ecosystems: A Multi-Level Analysis of Governance, Internationalization, Corporate Entrepreneurship, and Crowdfunding Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Prior research by Zahra and others has examined governance, internationalization, corporate entrepreneurship, and crowdfunding as distinct themes. However, in the contemporary (pre-2026) global landscape, these elements are increasingly interdependent within entrepreneurial ecosystems. Understanding their synergistic and conflicting interactions is critical for developing robust theoretical models and practical guidance for policymakers and firm leaders navigating complex, interconnected business environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance and resilience of entrepreneurial ecosystems are positively influenced by the strategic alignment and effective interface management between four core themes: governance structures, internationalization pathways, corporate entrepreneurship activities, and equity crowdfunding mechanisms.
proposals.method: This study employs a mixed-methods, multi-level design. First, a quantitative analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) on a proprietary panel dataset of 500 technology-based small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) from 2018-2024, measuring variables for each theme. Second, a qualitative comparative case study analysis of 15 firms will be conducted using NVivo software to code interview transcripts and archival data, examining the micro-processes of interface management. The integration of findings will utilize configurational analysis via fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) to identify necessary and sufficient conditions for ecosystem success.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide an integrative, multi-thematic framework that moves beyond siloed examinations of entrepreneurship topics. It will offer empirical evidence on how governance, international business, corporate venturing, and new financing mechanisms co-evolve, contributing to both the entrepreneurship and international business literatures. The findings will yield actionable insights for ecosystem architects on fostering productive thematic convergence.
proposals.novelty: While the individual themes are well-studied, this proposal's novelty lies in theorizing and empirically testing their dynamic interfaces and combined configurational effects within a unified ecosystem model, a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 scholarship.

proposals.title: Beyond the Survey: An Ethnographic Study of Lived Experience and Decision-Making at the Marketing-Entrepreneurship Interface in SMEs
proposals.motivation: Research on the marketing-entrepreneurship interface has traditionally relied on surveys and case studies, which can miss the nuanced, real-time, and socially embedded nature of entrepreneurial decision-making. Understanding this lived experience is crucial, as it shapes how small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) identify opportunities, interact with customers, and navigate market uncertainties, ultimately impacting their survival and growth. This gap limits our theoretical and practical understanding of how marketing is enacted in entrepreneurial contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep ethnographic immersion will reveal that SME entrepreneurs' marketing-related decisions are not purely analytical but are fundamentally shaped by tacit knowledge, identity work, and improvisational practices within their immediate social and material contexts.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-sited ethnographic approach, involving prolonged immersion (6-9 months) within 3-5 purposefully selected SMEs. Data collection will include participant observation of daily operations, marketing meetings, and customer interactions; in-depth, semi-structured interviews with founders and key employees; and analysis of artifacts (e.g., business plans, social media posts). Data will be analyzed using an iterative, abductive process, moving between empirical observations and existing theory, with techniques including thematic analysis and narrative construction to build thick descriptions of decision-making processes.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a rich, contextually grounded theory of entrepreneurial marketing-in-practice, moving beyond static models to capture its dynamic, emergent nature. It will offer novel insights for scholars of corporate entrepreneurship and international business by illuminating the micro-foundations of strategic adaptation. Practically, it will provide SME stakeholders with a deeper understanding of the human and social factors driving effective market engagement.
proposals.novelty: While ethnography is established in anthropology, its application to rigorously study the *marketing-entrepreneurship interface* within SMEs is novel, offering a methodological and theoretical departure from the dominant positivist approaches in the field. It directly addresses calls for deeper qualitative work to understand the 'how' and 'why' of entrepreneurial processes.

proposals.title: The Market-Made Entrepreneur: A Longitudinal Study of How Marketing Practices Constitute Entrepreneurial Identity
proposals.motivation: Research on the entrepreneur-marketing interface has predominantly focused on how an entrepreneur's pre-existing identity shapes their marketing strategies. This unidirectional view overlooks the constitutive role of marketing activities. Given the rise of personal branding, social media, and equity crowdfunding (where entrepreneurs market themselves to investors), understanding how these performative acts reflexively shape the entrepreneur's identity is a critical gap with implications for entrepreneurial learning, resilience, and venture governance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an entrepreneur's engagement in specific marketing practices—particularly narrative construction, stakeholder feedback loops, and personal brand management—triggers iterative identity work, leading to a professional identity that is substantively shaped by, rather than merely expressed through, their market-facing activities.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, multi-case qualitative design, tracking early-stage entrepreneurs for 18-24 months. Data collection includes bi-monthly interviews, ethnographic observation of marketing activities (e.g., pitch sessions, social media management), and analysis of marketing artifacts. Using Gioia methodology, we will code data iteratively to build a process model, specifically analyzing sequences where marketing actions precede shifts in self-concept, role perception, and entrepreneurial narratives, thereby mapping the inverse causal pathway.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute to corporate entrepreneurship and marketing theory by developing a model of identity co-construction, challenging the static view of entrepreneurial traits. Practically, it will offer insights for entrepreneurship education and investor relations, highlighting how governance mechanisms like crowdfunding can inadvertently reshape founder identity.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic assumption in entrepreneurial marketing, positing marketing as an identity-forming mechanism rather than an output of identity. It integrates Zahra's work on governance (e.g., crowdfunding) with identity theory, examining how external stakeholder engagement internalizes new role expectations.

proposals.title: Navigating the Tension: The Interplay of Entrepreneurial Action and Administrative Process in SME Marketing Decision-Making
proposals.motivation: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) are critical engines of innovation and economic growth, yet their marketing strategies are often caught between the need for entrepreneurial agility and the demands of administrative control. Prior to 2026, research has largely examined these marketing modes in isolation, creating a gap in understanding their dynamic interplay within the resource-constrained, high-uncertainty context of SMEs. This study addresses this gap by investigating how SME leaders manage this fundamental tension to achieve marketing effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the most effective SMEs are those that achieve a dynamic balance, or 'ambidexterity,' between entrepreneurial marketing (characterized by proactivity, innovation, and opportunity-seeking) and administrative marketing (characterized by systematization, analysis, and control), and that this balance is contingent upon the firm's lifecycle stage and competitive environment.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, we will conduct semi-structured interviews with 30-40 SME founders and marketing executives to map decision-making processes and identify key tension points. Second, building on these insights, we will develop and administer a large-scale survey (n≈400) to quantitatively test our hypotheses. The survey will use validated scales for entrepreneurial orientation, marketing planning formalization, and performance. Data analysis will involve thematic coding for qualitative data and structural equation modeling (SEM) using software like AMOS or Mplus to test the proposed contingent relationships.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a nuanced, integrative model of SME marketing that moves beyond simplistic either/or frameworks. It will offer actionable insights for SME managers on how to structure decision-making processes. For academia, it will extend Zahra's work on corporate entrepreneurship into the marketing domain and provide empirical evidence on the governance of entrepreneurial action within small business contexts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly framing and empirically testing the co-existence and interaction of entrepreneurial and administrative logics within the specific functional area of SME marketing, a critical interface previously underexplored in integrative terms.

proposals.title: Formalized Demand and Agro-Entrepreneurial Innovation: A Constraint-Based Theory of Market Logic in Food Systems
proposals.motivation: Global food security challenges demand systemic innovation beyond technical solutions. However, the dominant marketing paradigm in food systems, characterized by formal procurement standards and standardized value chain contracts, may create a critical but understudied constraint. This research addresses how these formal demand channels shape the very nature of entrepreneurial opportunity recognition and exploitation in agriculture, potentially marginalizing ventures crucial for resilience and sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: The formalization of market demand signals in food systems creates a 'market-logic constraint' that systematically biases agro-entrepreneurial innovation towards scalable, technical solutions and away from innovations integrating alternative management practices, social policy, or localized ecological knowledge.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, comparative case study design. First, we will conduct content analysis of procurement documents and contracts from major supermarket chains and agribusinesses to codify formal demand criteria. Second, we will use qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) on a sample of 40-60 agro-entrepreneurial ventures to identify configurations of venture characteristics (e.g., innovation type, governance, resource base) that are associated with successful access to formal channels versus reliance on alternative markets. Third, semi-structured interviews with entrepreneurs and channel gatekeepers will provide processual data on constraint enactment.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend corporate entrepreneurship theory by theorizing the external market-interface as a constraining, rather than merely enabling, structure. It will contribute to marketing theory by revealing how formal channel management actively shapes innovation upstream. Practically, it will provide policymakers and ecosystem builders with a framework to design demand-side interventions that mitigate these constraints.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines entrepreneurial entry into existing markets, this study flips the perspective to theorize how the marketing interface itself disciplines and constrains the genesis of entrepreneurial ventures, particularly in the critical context of food system transformation.

proposals.title: Beyond Generic Toolkits: A Comparative Case Study of Coping Mechanisms in Food-Insecure Households and Resource-Constrained SMEs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in food security critiques universal coping strategies, while entrepreneurship literature documents strategic differences between SMEs and large firms. Yet, a significant gap exists in understanding how fundamentally different units—households and firms—adapt to analogous resource scarcity and volatility. This parallel is critical because both domains are subject to one-size-fits-all policy interventions (e.g., standardized social safety nets, generic SME support programs) that may fail due to a lack of contextual fit.
proposals.hypothesis: The effectiveness of coping mechanisms under resource scarcity is contingent not on a universal set of strategies, but on the strategic fit between the specific organizational logic (household subsistence vs. entrepreneurial venture), localized resource endowments, and the nature of the exogenous shock.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative, multiple-case study design across varied economic and cultural settings (e.g., a developed economy post-2008 recession, an emerging market). Data collection will involve in-depth interviews, ethnographic observation, and archival analysis for matched pairs of food-insecure households and resource-constrained SMEs within the same community. Analysis will use pattern-matching logic and cross-case synthesis to map contingent strategic logics, employing software like NVivo for thematic coding and comparative display.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a mid-range theory of contextual coping, challenging universal policy models. It will directly contribute to the entrepreneurship literature by extending the understanding of strategic heterogeneity under constraints, and to social policy by demonstrating the need for tailored interventions. The cross-domain comparison offers a novel lens for both fields.
proposals.novelty: The study's novelty lies in its explicit, empirical comparison of coping mechanisms across the household and firm organizational forms, synthesizing two distinct literature streams to challenge universalist assumptions in both policy and academic theory.

proposals.title: Grassroots Resilience: An Ethnographic Study of Entrepreneurial Adaptation and Local Knowledge Systems in Food-Insecure Communities
proposals.motivation: Food insecurity remains a critical global challenge, exacerbated by climate volatility and economic shocks. Traditional policy interventions often treat affected communities as passive recipients of aid, overlooking their capacity for entrepreneurial adaptation. This research addresses this gap by investigating the grassroots economic and social processes through which vulnerable populations actively navigate and mitigate food scarcity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that food-insecure communities develop and rely on sophisticated, informal entrepreneurial systems—characterized by unique social networks, local knowledge exchange, and adaptive business practices—that constitute a primary mechanism of resilience, and that these systems are often more effective and sustainable than externally imposed solutions.
proposals.method: This study employs a multi-sited, longitudinal ethnographic approach. The primary method is deep participant observation within 3-4 distinct communities in the U.S. identified as high-risk for food insecurity. This will be supplemented by 40-50 in-depth, semi-structured interviews with informal entrepreneurs (e.g., urban gardeners, informal food vendors, community kitchen organizers) and systematic narrative collection to document decision-making logics. Data analysis will follow an iterative, grounded theory approach, using NVivo software for thematic coding to identify patterns in adaptive strategies, network structures, and knowledge flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a process-oriented theory of entrepreneurial resilience from the ground up, enriching the corporate entrepreneurship literature by extending it to informal, community-based contexts. It will generate actionable insights for policymakers and NGOs, providing a framework for designing interventions that support, rather than displace, existing community-driven economic adaptations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying deep ethnography—a method rarely used in mainstream entrepreneurship research—to systematically study informal entrepreneurial activity as a core resilience mechanism in food insecurity, shifting the analytical lens from vulnerability to agentic adaptation.

proposals.title: Bridging Structure and Cognition: A Dual-Method Analysis of Policy Entrepreneurship in Global Food Security Governance
proposals.motivation: Global food security governance is a complex adaptive system facing unprecedented challenges from climate change, geopolitical instability, and supply chain disruptions. Understanding how policy change occurs requires moving beyond static network maps to examine the interplay between actors' relational positions and their cognitive frameworks. This research addresses a critical gap by investigating how policy entrepreneurs navigate this interface to open policy windows and drive systemic adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: The effectiveness of a policy entrepreneur in influencing food security governance is a function of the alignment between their structural network position (e.g., centrality, brokerage) and the strategic deployment of their cognitive schema (e.g., causal beliefs, solution frames) to mobilize allies and resources.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, we will construct a multi-level network of formal and informal ties among key actors (NGOs, agencies, firms) using archival data and surveys, analyzing structure with UCINET for metrics like betweenness centrality. Second, we will conduct in-depth interviews with identified entrepreneurs to build cognitive maps using causal mapping techniques and software like Decision Explorer. The fusion will involve quantitative analysis (e.g., QAP regression) to test for correlations between network metrics and cognitive map characteristics (e.g., complexity, centrality of certain beliefs).
proposals.contribution: This study contributes to corporate and policy entrepreneurship literature by providing an integrated model of agency and structure in complex governance. It offers a novel methodological framework for simultaneously analyzing network and cognitive data, yielding practical insights for entrepreneurs and policymakers on how to strategically navigate food security challenges.
proposals.novelty: While network and cognitive analyses exist separately, their synthesis to explain entrepreneurial action in policy systems is novel. This approach moves beyond describing 'who knows whom' or 'what they think' to model how relational structure and cognition co-evolve to create entrepreneurial opportunities.

proposals.title: From Surplus to Strategy: A Resource-Based View of Marketing Food Waste in SMEs
proposals.motivation: Globally, one-third of food produced is wasted, representing a massive environmental and economic inefficiency. For SMEs, this waste is a significant cost, yet food insecurity remains a critical societal challenge. Current research treats waste management and marketing as separate functions, missing the strategic opportunity to reconceptualize surplus as a unique, firm-specific resource that can address both business and social goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SMEs which strategically bundle their idiosyncratic food surplus resources (e.g., imperfect produce, by-products) and design targeted marketing campaigns directly from these resource bundles will achieve superior outcomes in brand differentiation, customer loyalty, and revenue diversification compared to those using standard, generic waste reduction practices.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods design. First, qualitative case studies of 15-20 pioneering SMEs will be conducted to identify resource bundling and marketing constraint strategies. Second, a quantitative survey of 300+ food sector SMEs will be distributed. Data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the relationships between resource heterogeneity, marketing strategy specificity, and firm performance, controlling for firm size and sector.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, integrative framework at the interface of corporate entrepreneurship and marketing, showing how SMEs can entrepreneurially leverage waste. It will extend the Resource-Based View into the domain of sustainability marketing and provide actionable strategies for turning a cost center into a source of competitive advantage and social impact.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating a firm's specific food surplus not as a homogeneous problem but as a heterogeneous, marketable resource bundle, and in framing the marketing strategy as a direct, constrained function of these idiosyncratic resources—an inverse approach to typical demand-driven marketing.

proposals.title: Converging Motivations: A Comparative Analysis of Entrepreneurial and Corporate Managerial Drivers in Innovation Contexts
proposals.motivation: The entrepreneurship literature has long posited that entrepreneurs possess fundamentally distinct motivations (e.g., autonomy, innovation, wealth creation) compared to corporate managers (e.g., stability, promotion, resource stewardship). This assumed dichotomy influences theory, policy, and corporate venturing practices. However, in the pre-2026 context of blurred organizational boundaries, the rise of intrapreneurship, and equity crowdfunding, this core assumption requires empirical testing to understand if motivations are truly divergent or contextually convergent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when controlling for context (e.g., leading a new product initiative), the motivational profiles of independent entrepreneurs and corporate managers will show significant convergence, particularly in dimensions of achievement, innovation, and risk propensity, challenging the foundational assumption of inherent difference.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method, matched-pair design. First, we will conduct semi-structured interviews to identify key motivational constructs. Second, we will administer a large-scale survey to two matched samples: independent entrepreneurs leading early-stage ventures and corporate managers leading similar-scale new product development projects. Data analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test for measurement invariance and compare latent mean scores across groups on core motivational dimensions, controlling for demographic and contextual variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute to corporate entrepreneurship and strategic management by providing a nuanced, empirically grounded view of motivational drivers. It will refine theories of entrepreneurial behavior by identifying contextual rather than inherent motivational boundaries. Practically, it will inform the design of corporate venturing programs and founder-manager recruitment by highlighting shared drivers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing a foundational, often untested, assumption in entrepreneurship research by comparing matched groups in similar innovation contexts, moving beyond descriptive typologies to a rigorous comparative analysis. It integrates Zahra's work on corporate entrepreneurship and governance (e.g., equity crowdfunding) to examine the 'interface' of entrepreneurial and managerial roles.

proposals.title: Resource Scarcity as a Catalyst: An RBV Analysis of Constraint-Driven Marketing Innovation in SMEs
proposals.motivation: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) face significant marketing constraints due to limited financial, human, and technological resources. The Resource-Based View (RBV), a dominant strategy framework, has been underexplored in marketing contexts, particularly for understanding how SMEs innovate under scarcity. This gap is critical as SMEs are vital for economic dynamism and job creation, and their ability to leverage unique, intangible resources for marketing innovation remains poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SMEs' marketing constraints do not merely limit activities but actively shape the development and deployment of valuable, rare, and inimitable (VRIO) intangible resources, such as entrepreneurial bricolage and community-based relational capital, which in turn drive novel, effective marketing strategies.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method, longitudinal design. First, a quantitative survey of 300-500 SME founders will measure perceived marketing constraints, resource configurations (using established RBV/VRIO scales), and marketing innovation outcomes. Structural equation modeling (SEM) will test the hypothesized relationships. Second, a qualitative multiple-case study of 8-12 purposefully selected SMEs will provide in-depth, processual insights into how constraints trigger resource recombination and bricolage in real-time marketing decisions.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, constraint-centric theoretical model to the marketing-entrepreneurship interface, moving beyond a deficit perspective of SME marketing. It will offer practical, evidence-based guidance for SME managers on strategically framing and leveraging constraints. It also extends the RBV by applying it to the tactical, market-facing activities where resources are ultimately validated.
proposals.novelty: While RBV is well-established in strategy, its application to explain marketing behavior under constraint is novel. The study uniquely posits constraints not as barriers but as a conditional context that shapes the very development of strategic marketing resources, integrating Zahra's work on corporate entrepreneurship with marketing theory.

proposals.title: Marketing at the Strategic Interface: A Configurational Analysis of Marketing Practices in High-Growth vs. Lifestyle-Oriented SMEs
proposals.motivation: The SME sector is not monolithic, yet marketing research often treats it as such, overlooking fundamental strategic differences. Understanding how marketing practices diverge between high-growth entrepreneurial SMEs and lifestyle-oriented SMEs is critical for developing tailored theories and practical support. This gap is especially pertinent in a pre-2026 context where digital platforms and crowdfunding have reshaped marketing resource constraints and opportunities for both types of firms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-growth and lifestyle SMEs will exhibit distinct, internally coherent configurations of marketing practices, resources, and strategic intent. Specifically, high-growth SMEs will integrate market-driving, innovation-centric practices with formalized opportunity scanning, while lifestyle SMEs will employ market-responsive, relationship-centric practices focused on stability and local reputation.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods, comparative case study design. First, we will conduct a Q-sort analysis with a panel of SME owners and experts to identify core dimensions of SME marketing practice. Subsequently, we will select 40-50 SMEs (balanced between high-growth and lifestyle) for in-depth, semi-structured interviews and analysis of marketing artifacts. Data will be analyzed using fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) to identify necessary and sufficient conditions and causal configurations that distinguish the two strategic archetypes.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a nuanced, configurational theory of SME marketing that moves beyond one-size-fits-all models. It will provide empirical evidence on the 'marketing-entrepreneurship interface' by linking strategic intent to specific marketing practice bundles. Practically, it will offer clearer diagnostic tools for entrepreneurs and advisors to align marketing activities with core strategic goals.
proposals.novelty: While prior work distinguishes SME types, this study novelly applies a configurational, fsQCA approach to marketing practices, moving from simple correlations to identifying causal recipes. It directly addresses Zahra's call to explore the 'interface' between entrepreneurship and marketing within the heterogeneous SME context.

ideas.title: Invert the focus from entrepreneurship to marketing's contribution.
ideas.description: Study what marketing theory can contribute to entrepreneurship, not just entrepreneurship to marketing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2004: Proceedings of the Twenty-Fourth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Relax the need for a single entrepreneurship definition
ideas.description: Study the interface without a universal definition, using a pluralistic or context-dependent view of entrepreneurship.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2005 : Proceedings of the Twenty-Fifth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Map Marketing's Contribution to Entrepreneurial Success
ideas.description: This research inverts the focus from entrepreneurship to marketing's contribution by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, the analogous problem of understanding an organism's success was solved by developing niche theory, which maps how the organism fits into and exploits its specific environmental conditions, rather than just how the environment shapes it. Adapted to the research context, this means shifting from studying how entrepreneurial traits shape marketing to mapping the specific 'marketing niche'—the combination of market conditions, customer behaviors, and communication landscapes—that a venture must identify and exploit. The adapted idea is to develop a framework, based on marketing theory, to classify these entrepreneurial marketing niches and prescribe the specific marketing knowledge, strategies, and practices required for a new venture to successfully occupy and defend its chosen niche, thereby clarifying marketing's foundational contribution to entrepreneurial viability.

ideas.title: Dual: Compare entrepreneurial and administrative marketing in SMEs
ideas.description: Study the dual forces of entrepreneurial action versus administrative process within SME marketing decision-making.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2005 : Proceedings of the Twenty-Fifth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Mapping the Configurational Space of Entrepreneurship: A Computational and Empirical Framework for Interdisciplinary Grounding
ideas.description: This research program rejects the feasibility and utility of a single operational definition. Instead, it empirically maps the multi-dimensional conceptual space of 'entrepreneurship' as enacted in the marketing and entrepreneurship interface literature and adjacent practitioner discourse. Using computational text mining and natural language processing on a large corpus of relevant publications, it identifies the core constituent dimensions (e.g., innovation, risk, value creation, opportunity) and their frequent configurations. These empirically derived configurations are then validated and refined through structured elicitation workshops with scholars from both fields. The result is a flexible, evidence-based framework that allows researchers to explicitly state which configuration of entrepreneurial dimensions their work addresses, providing a far more robust and precise grounding for interdisciplinary studies.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: How marketing shapes entrepreneurial identity
ideas.description: Examine the inverse relationship: how marketing activities and roles define or reshape the entrepreneur's own identity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2005 : Proceedings of the Twenty-Fifth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Relax the assumption that SMEs lack effective marketing.
ideas.description: Study SMEs that excel at marketing despite resource constraints, challenging the deficit model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2004: Proceedings of the Twenty-Fourth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Dual: Compare high-growth vs lifestyle SME marketing
ideas.description: Contrast marketing practices between entrepreneurial SMEs seeking high growth and lifestyle SMEs seeking stability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2008: Proceedings of the Twenty-eighth Annual Entrepreneurship Conference

ideas.title: Evaluate the 'Leading for Growth' pilot programme
ideas.description: Assess the impact of the pilot leadership programme on SME resilience and competitiveness post-Brexit.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.title: Governance of Marketing Excellence in SMEs: Formal Contracts, Informal Norms, and Resourceful Capability
ideas.description: This research investigates how small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) that excel at marketing govern this critical function, specifically examining the interplay between formal contractual mechanisms (e.g., agency agreements, performance clauses) and informal relational norms (e.g., trust, shared values, community reputation) within and around the firm. It challenges the dual deficit model that assumes SMEs both lack marketing skill and rely on simplistic, informal governance, proposing instead that high-performing SMEs strategically and resourcefully blend formal and informal governance to build a dynamic, difficult-to-imitate marketing capability. The study would explore how this tailored governance mix enables excellence despite constraints, and how shifts in the formal-informal balance drive or sustain marketing performance over time.

ideas.title: Defining Entrepreneurship via a Diagnostic Framework: An Analogy from Clinical Psychology
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from clinical psychology to the problem of defining entrepreneurship. The analogous problem—defining complex, multifaceted constructs like mental disorders for reliable diagnosis and research—was solved through operationalized diagnostic criteria (e.g., DSM-5), which specify a checklist of observable symptoms and a minimum threshold for diagnosis. Adapted to entrepreneurship, this approach proposes developing a robust operational definition not as a single essence, but as a diagnostic framework. Entrepreneurship would be defined as an activity diagnosed by the presence of a critical mass of measurable behavioral and economic 'symptoms' (e.g., novelty, resource assembly, value creation intent, risk-bearing). This diagnostic definition provides the clear, actionable, and consistent grounding needed for empirical research at the marketing/entrepreneurship interface, allowing scholars to reliably identify and study entrepreneurial phenomena.

ideas.title: Use computational text analysis on entrepreneurship proceedings.
ideas.description: Analyze symposium proceedings text to trace the evolution of core interface concepts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2004: Proceedings of the Twenty-Fourth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Apply ethnographic methods from anthropology to SME studies
ideas.description: Use deep ethnographic immersion to understand the lived experience and decision-making of entrepreneurs at the marketing interface.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2005 : Proceedings of the Twenty-Fifth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Entrepreneurial Heterogeneity in SME Marketing within Food Systems
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the study of entrepreneur-specific motivations and skills with agent-based modeling (ABM) to create a computational laboratory for food systems. It models a population of heterogeneous SME agents, each endowed with distinct motivational archetypes and marketing skill sets, operating within a simulated food system featuring suppliers, distributors, regulators, and consumers. By running simulations under various shocks (e.g., supply chain disruptions, policy changes), the model can reveal how different entrepreneurial types drive the adoption of specific marketing innovations (like direct-to-consumer channels or sustainability branding) and collectively influence the system's overall adaptability and economic outcomes. This approach moves beyond correlational studies to test causal mechanisms linking micro-level entrepreneurial psychology to macro-level system dynamics.

ideas.title: The Market-Logic Constraint: How Formalized Demand Channels Stifle Agro-Entrepreneurial Innovation
ideas.description: This research investigates how the formal structures and processes used to define, measure, and signal market demand in food systems—such as supermarket procurement standards, formal value chain contracts, and standardized marketing frameworks—actively constrain agro-entrepreneurial innovation. It posits that this 'market-logic constraint' forces food security challenges to be addressed primarily through predictable, scalable technical solutions that fit existing commercial channels, thereby marginalizing entrepreneurial ventures based on alternative management practices, social policy integration, or localized ecological knowledge. The core argument is that the dominant marketing paradigm creates a dual problem: it stifles the entrepreneurial experimentation necessary for systemic resilience and simultaneously reinforces the misconception that food security is a purely technical problem, decoupled from managerial and policy innovation.

ideas.title: The Corporate Immune System: Studying Immunological Tolerance as a Model for Entrepreneurial Marketing in Large Firms
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, this study examines how large corporations manage the dual challenge of fostering entrepreneurial marketing behaviors while inhibiting disruptive or misaligned activities, much like an immune system must tolerate 'self' while attacking 'non-self.' The analogous solution from immunology is the concept of 'immunological tolerance'—a set of active mechanisms that prevent the immune system from attacking the body's own tissues. Adapted to the research context, the study will investigate the corporate equivalent: the formal and informal systems, processes, and cultural norms that act as 'tolerance mechanisms' within large firms. The research will identify how these mechanisms (e.g., specific review gates, mentorship programs, defined 'sandbox' environments, and cultural narratives) selectively permit or suppress entrepreneurial marketing initiatives within divisions. The goal is to develop a framework explaining how large organizations can scale entrepreneurial marketing without triggering destructive 'autoimmune' responses that stifle innovation or, conversely, without losing strategic control.

ideas.title: A Configurational and Causal Analysis of Marketing Logics: Moving Beyond Firm Size Dichotomies
ideas.description: This research program challenges the simplistic SME vs. large firm dichotomy by proposing that distinct marketing logics arise from specific configurations of internal capabilities, external constraints, and strategic orientations, not from size alone. It first employs fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) on cross-sectional survey and archival data to identify the necessary and sufficient condition sets that predict the adoption of different marketing practices. Subsequently, it tests the causal efficacy of these configurations through a series of carefully matched, in-depth longitudinal case studies, tracking real-time decision-making and performance outcomes. This design directly isolates the mechanisms through which context drives marketing action, yielding more actionable and theoretically robust insights for both entrepreneurs and policymakers.

ideas.title: Testing Entrepreneurial Marketing as a Distinct Behavioral Phenotype, Not a Resource-Constrained Adaptation
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to challenge the assumption that entrepreneurship is distinct from SME management. The analogous problem in biology is distinguishing a specialized behavioral adaptation from a generalist response to environmental constraints (like scarcity). Biologists solve this using comparative phylogenetic methods, mapping traits onto evolutionary trees to identify independent emergence in specific lineages, controlling for shared ancestry and general constraints. Adapted to the research context, this involves constructing a firm typology or 'lineage' based on founding conditions (e.g., innovative vs. traditional opportunity, growth intent). The study would then test if a suite of entrepreneurial marketing behaviors consistently clusters as a distinct 'phenotype' within a specific entrepreneurial lineage, using methods that control for the universal variable of resource constraints common to all SMEs. A positive finding would suggest these behaviors are a unique adaptation to uncertainty and creation, not merely a scaled-down version of marketing for any small or constrained firm, fundamentally challenging the distinction between entrepreneurship and SME management.

ideas.title: Apply resource-based view from strategy to marketing constraints
ideas.description: Analyze SME marketing through the lens of the Resource-Based View to understand constraint-driven innovation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2008: Proceedings of the Twenty-eighth Annual Entrepreneurship Conference

ideas.title: Causal Mechanisms: Isolating how specific corporate systems drive or destroy entrepreneurial marketing outcomes.
ideas.description: This research employs a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design to establish causal links between corporate systems and entrepreneurial marketing efficacy. It identifies matched pairs of divisions or initiative teams within large firms and tracks the lifecycle of specific entrepreneurial marketing projects, measuring inputs, organizational interventions, and hard performance outcomes. By combining longitudinal quantitative analysis of initiative survival and growth with in-depth process tracing, the study isolates the specific mechanisms (e.g., dynamic governance, protected resource pools, metric systems) that causally enable or kill valuable entrepreneurial behaviors, providing actionable design principles for corporate systems.

ideas.title: Assume SME motivations are similar to large firms.
ideas.description: Investigate if growth and profit motivations in SMEs are truly different from corporate managers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2004: Proceedings of the Twenty-Fourth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Marketing Theory as Diagnostic Framework: Explaining and Mitigating Specific Entrepreneurial Failure Modes
ideas.description: This research program inverts the typical flow of knowledge by treating core marketing theory as a diagnostic toolkit for entrepreneurial pathology. It begins by empirically identifying persistent, costly failure modes in new ventures, such as faulty market segmentation or value communication breakdowns. It then investigates which established marketing constructs (e.g., brand resonance, service-dominant logic, customer journey modeling) can best diagnose the root causes of these failures and, crucially, predict their onset. The outcome is a validated, actionable framework that specifies which marketing theories contribute to entrepreneurship, under what conditions, and through what causal mechanisms, moving beyond generic application to prescriptive utility.

ideas.title: Applying Ecosystem Engineering Principles to SME Marketing Resource Bundles
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying the resource-based view (RBV) from strategic management to analyze SME marketing constraints by focusing on their unique, bundled resources rather than standard practices. Through analogical reasoning, it draws a parallel from the field of Ecology, specifically Ecosystem Engineering, where organisms are studied for how their unique traits bundle resources to create novel niches, not compared to a generic ideal. The analogous solution involves mapping trait-resource interactions to understand emergent functionality. Adapted to the research context, the idea is to conceptualize an SME as a 'marketing ecosystem engineer.' The research would develop a framework to diagnostically map how an SME's specific, often unconventional, bundle of resources (e.g., owner's social capital, deep community ties, specialized craft skills) interacts to engineer a unique market position and value delivery system. This approach moves the focus from adopting standard marketing practices to identifying, strengthening, and exploiting these firm-specific, engineered resource configurations as the core of SME marketing strategy.

ideas.title: Ethnographic Study of Entrepreneurial Adaptation in Food-Insecure Communities
ideas.description: This research applies deep ethnographic immersion—participant observation, in-depth interviews, and narrative collection—to study the lived experience and decision-making of informal entrepreneurs and community organizers within populations identified as high-risk for food insecurity. Instead of viewing these groups solely as vulnerable recipients, the approach investigates them as active agents of adaptation, mapping the grassroots economic innovations, social networks, and local knowledge systems they employ to secure food. The synergy yields a rich, process-oriented understanding of resilience and vulnerability, generating insights for policies and interventions that support, rather than disrupt, existing community-driven solutions.

ideas.title: Test the model in different industry contexts.
ideas.description: Empirically examine the interaction of operating and dynamic capabilities across various sectors and competitive environments.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Evolving Firm: How Dynamic and Operating Capabilities Interact to Enable Entrepreneurship

ideas.title: Explore the four themes from the published work further
ideas.description: Extend the investigation of the four thematic areas identified in the author's twenty-three published submissions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2005 : Proceedings of the Twenty-Fifth Annual Entrepreneurship Research Conference

ideas.title: Applying Behavioral Ecology's Foraging Theory to Model SME Motivational Strategies
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that SME motivations are similar to large firms. Using an analogy from behavioral ecology, specifically foraging theory, it adapts the solution to animal patch-leaving decisions. Foraging theory was solved by modeling strategic trade-offs between exploiting known resources and exploring for new ones based on environmental context. Adapted to the research context, this leads to an investigation that models SME owner motivations as dynamic strategies. Instead of assuming static preferences, the study would identify the specific 'resource patches' (like market niches) and 'environmental constraints' (like owner's time or access to capital) that predict strategic shifts between profit-focused 'exploitation' and growth-focused 'exploration.' This approach reframes the core question from 'Are motivations different?' to 'Under what conditions do optimal motivational strategies diverge between SMEs and large firms?'

ideas.title: From Resource Audit to Constraint-Bundling: A Dynamic RBV Framework for SME Marketing Scarcity
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a practical framework that moves beyond the descriptive application of the Resource-Based View (RBV) to SME marketing. It first establishes a diagnostic tool for SMEs to systematically audit and score their marketing-relevant resources, including often-overlooked intangible assets like community relationships and employee expertise. The core of the framework is a set of evidence-based 'Constraint-Bundling Protocols' that provide specific, low-cost recipes for combining scarce resources in novel ways to overcome defined marketing execution gaps, thereby translating static resource analysis into dynamic, actionable strategy.

ideas.title: Cognitive Mapping of Entrepreneurial Sensemaking in Global Food Security Narratives
ideas.description: This research investigates how entrepreneurs and innovators in the agri-food sector construct cognitive maps to make strategic decisions under the profound uncertainty of food security challenges. It specifically examines how these individual cognitive frameworks are formed and adapted in response to dominant global narratives (e.g., 'climate-smart agriculture', 'localism', 'protein transition') propagated by media and international institutions. The synergy enables a dual analysis: how narratives constrain or enable entrepreneurial cognition, and how entrepreneurs' cognitive interpretations and actions feed back to reinforce, resist, or reshape the narratives themselves, offering a dynamic model of agency within discursive systems.

ideas.title: Resource-Based Marketing of Food Surplus: A Strategy for SME Food Security and Waste Reduction
ideas.description: This research investigates how small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in the food sector can apply the Resource-Based View (RBV) from strategic management to reconceptualize and market their food surplus and waste. It moves beyond standard waste management practices by analyzing a firm's specific excess food streams—whether imperfect produce, by-products, or logistical overages—as a unique, bundled resource that can be leveraged in targeted marketing strategies. The core inquiry is how SMEs can design marketing constraints and campaigns that are a direct function of these idiosyncratic surplus resources, thereby addressing the inverse problem of food security (managing excess) while simultaneously building brand value, customer loyalty, and new revenue models. This transforms waste system management from a cost center into an integral, strategic component of marketing and competitive differentiation.

ideas.title: Mapping Configurations of SME Motivations: A Typology Beyond the Growth-Profit Binary
ideas.description: This research rejects the assumption that SME motivations can be simplistically compared to large firms. Instead, it constructs a multi-dimensional framework capturing economic, behavioral, and personal drivers. Using sequential mixed methods—initial interviews to refine dimensions, followed by a large-scale survey—it applies cluster analysis and Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) to identify distinct, coherent archetypes of SME owner-managers (e.g., 'lifestyle sustainers,' 'legacy builders,' 'constrained growth-seekers'). The study then tests how these motivational configurations predict strategic choices and firm performance, providing a nuanced, empirically grounded typology that explains heterogeneity within the SME sector.

ideas.title: Scale up: Compare the East Midlands to other UK regions
ideas.description: Benchmark East Midlands SME challenges and strategies against those in the North West or South East.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.title: Applying Allometric Scaling Principles to SME vs. Large Firm Marketing
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from biological allometric scaling to empirically test differences between SME and large firm marketing. In biology, allometry solves the comparison problem between organisms of different sizes by modeling how functions scale non-linearly with mass (e.g., metabolic rate scales to the 3/4 power). The analogous solution is to identify universal scaling laws rather than just categorical differences. Adapted to the business context, the study would collect data on firm size (the independent scaling variable) and a suite of marketing process metrics (dependent variables). The goal is to move beyond validating broad claims of different circumstances to discovering the specific mathematical relationships (scaling exponents and constants) that govern how marketing efficiency, cost, agility, and other functions change with firm size. This provides a predictive, empirical framework for understanding marketing constraints and advantages across the entire size spectrum.

ideas.title: Deconstructing Marketing Efficacy: A Matched-Pair, Longitudinal Study of Practice and Performance in Resource-Constrained SMEs
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple excellence model to systematically compare high, average, and low-performing SMEs within tightly matched industry and resource constraints. Using a longitudinal, mixed-methods approach, it tracks marketing practices, strategic decisions, and adaptive behaviors over 18-24 months. The design explicitly tests for causal mechanisms separating effective from ineffective marketing, controlling for sector and resource context, to generate actionable frameworks rather than just inspirational case studies. The outcome is a contingency model of SME marketing efficacy that identifies necessary and sufficient practices for given constraint profiles.

ideas.title: Relax assumption that entrepreneurs seek active governance
ideas.description: Research entrepreneurs who resist investor governance, viewing the crowd as passive capital.
ideas.sourceTitle: Equity Crowdfunding and Governance: Toward an Integrative Model and Research Agenda

ideas.title: Contextual Scarcity and Strategic Heterogeneity: A Comparative Study of Coping Mechanisms in Food-Insecure Households and Resource-Constrained SMEs
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the critique of universal coping strategies in food security with the empirical testing of strategic differences between SMEs and large firms. It investigates the parallel coping mechanisms employed by food-insecure households and small, resource-constrained enterprises when facing analogous threats to their viability, such as income shocks or market volatility. By conducting comparative, context-rich case studies across varied economic and cultural settings, the project will map the contingent logic of adaptive strategies, challenging one-size-fits-all policy interventions in both social safety nets and SME support programs. The core insight is that effective coping is not defined by a generic toolkit but by the strategic fit between localized resources, constraints, and the specific organizational logic of the unit under stress.

ideas.title: Test substitutability for other tech sectors
ideas.description: Investigate if VC and credit are substitutes for entrepreneurship in AI, biotech, or clean energy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Formal institutions, culture, and venture capital activity: A cross-country analysis

ideas.title: Track SME vulnerability and resilience metrics over time
ideas.description: Longitudinally measure how Brexit-related changes affect the strengths and vulnerabilities of micro and small firms.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.title: Challenge that entrepreneurial motivations are always different
ideas.description: Test if entrepreneur motivations are fundamentally distinct from corporate manager motivations in similar contexts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2008: Proceedings of the Twenty-eighth Annual Entrepreneurship Conference

ideas.title: Triangulating the Evolution of Entrepreneurial Concepts Through Multi-Genre Computational Text Analysis
ideas.description: This research traces the evolution of core interface concepts in entrepreneurship by computationally analyzing and comparing textual trajectories across multiple, distinct genres. It builds a corpus spanning symposium proceedings, venture capital investment memos, technology journalism, and startup post-mortem blogs to mitigate the performative and consensus bias of any single source. By modeling semantic networks and concept adoption curves across these genres, the system identifies divergences between scholarly discourse, investment logic, public narrative, and practical failure. This triangulation reveals a more robust and contested history of concept evolution, distinguishing rhetorical adoption from real-world implementation and abandonment.

ideas.title: Invert the focus from firm weaknesses to hidden strengths
ideas.description: Study how micro-firms' informal networks and adaptability confer unmeasured competitive advantages post-Brexit.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.title: Comparative Constrained Ethnography for Pattern Discovery in Entrepreneurial Behavior
ideas.description: This method synchronizes deep ethnographic immersion with multiple entrepreneurs operating under comparable constraints (e.g., seed-stage tech founders) using a shared, pre-defined observation framework. Researchers capture detailed data on specific, high-stakes routines—such as investor negotiations, product pivots, or team conflict resolution—across all cases. The resulting comparative analysis isolates recurring cognitive and social patterns from individual idiosyncrasies, transforming rich qualitative data into evidence for mid-range theories of entrepreneurial decision-making. This approach balances ethnographic depth with the analytical rigor needed for generalizable insight.

ideas.title: Causal Archetypes: Disentangling Founder Motivations, Dynamic Skill Development, and Marketing Trajectories in New Ventures
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static correlations by first empirically deriving founder motivational archetypes from a large baseline survey, rejecting a priori categorization. It then tracks a cohort of new ventures longitudinally, measuring founders' deliberate skill acquisition and actual marketing behaviors (e.g., resource allocation, channel adoption, branding choices) at multiple stages. By comparing these trajectories to those of hired managers in similar firms and controlling for environmental factors, the study isolates the causal impact of distinct founder archetypes on strategic marketing pathways, explaining not just what they do, but how and why their capabilities and choices evolve differently over time.

ideas.title: Develop a unified business leadership voice for the region
ideas.description: Design and test models for creating a single, effective advocacy body for East Midlands businesses.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Momentary Assessment from Clinical Psychology to Study Entrepreneurial Stress and Decision-Making
ideas.description: This idea adapts Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), a method from clinical psychology used to study dynamic processes like stress and coping in real-world settings, to the study of entrepreneurs. The analogous solution involves using smartphone prompts to collect repeated, in-the-moment data on behaviors and experiences, minimizing recall bias and capturing context. The adapted research idea employs a mobile application to prompt entrepreneurs several times daily over an extended period, recording their immediate context, affective state, resource constraints, and recent small-scale decisions. This method generates a high-resolution, temporal map of the entrepreneurial journey, allowing researchers to analyze the precursors to critical decisions, the real-time impact of stressors, and the micro-processes of opportunity evaluation and venture adaptation as they naturally occur.

ideas.title: Challenge the necessity of value network collaboration.
ideas.description: Study firms that build absorptive capacity and reconfigure capabilities entirely through internal processes.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Evolving Firm: How Dynamic and Operating Capabilities Interact to Enable Entrepreneurship

ideas.title: Scale down: Governance in very small ECF rounds
ideas.description: Study if and how governance emerges in campaigns with just a handful of micro-investors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Equity Crowdfunding and Governance: Toward an Integrative Model and Research Agenda

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Text Analysis of Entrepreneurship Proceedings
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary biology—phylogenetic analysis—to the context of computational text analysis of entrepreneurship proceedings. In biology, phylogenetic methods are used to reconstruct evolutionary trees from genetic data, tracing the history of traits. The analogous solution is to treat the textual data from annual or biannual symposium proceedings as a temporal sequence, with core interface concepts (e.g., 'platform,' 'ecosystem,' 'lean startup') operationalized as discrete or continuous character states. Computational phylogenetic algorithms (e.g., parsimony, likelihood, or Bayesian methods adapted for text) are then applied to infer the evolutionary relationships among these concepts, model their ancestral states, and pinpoint key moments of conceptual divergence, hybridization, or extinction. This provides a rigorous, model-based method for tracing the evolution of core concepts, moving beyond simple frequency tracking to infer the structure and dynamics of conceptual change within the entrepreneurship field.

ideas.title: Cognitive-Network Analysis of Policy Entrepreneurship in Food Security Governance
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network analysis and cognitive mapping to study policy entrepreneurs within food security governance systems. It maps the formal and informal relational networks among actors while simultaneously modeling the cognitive frameworks (e.g., causal beliefs, opportunity perceptions, risk assessments) key entrepreneurs use to make strategic decisions under uncertainty. The integrated approach analyzes how an actor's network position influences their cognitive schema and, conversely, how their unique cognitive model drives their networking behavior and strategy diffusion, ultimately explaining how policy windows open and close in complex adaptive systems.

ideas.title: Compare LEP productivity strategies across three counties
ideas.description: Analyze the relative effectiveness of different Local Enterprise Partnership strategies in Nottinghamshire, Derbyshire, and Leicestershire.
