ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.description: Study how nascent teams develop operating and dynamic capabilities simultaneously from inception.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Evolving Firm: How Dynamic and Operating Capabilities Interact to Enable Entrepreneurship

ideas.title: Apply cognitive mapping from psychology to entrepreneur decision-making
ideas.description: Use cognitive mapping techniques to model how entrepreneurs perceive and navigate marketing decisions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Frontiers of Entrepreneurship Research 2008: Proceedings of the Twenty-eighth Annual Entrepreneurship Conference

ideas.title: Assume endogenous and exogenous changes are intertwined.

ideas.description: Investigate if factors like supply chain agility or digital adoption matter more than leadership for SME resilience.

ideas.description: Identify policy interventions that would most degrade productivity and resilience in the micro-firm sector.

ideas.title: Tracing the Conceptual Evolution of Surplus and Waste in Food Security Discourse
ideas.description: This research applies computational text analysis (e.g., topic modeling, semantic network analysis) to the proceedings, white papers, and pitch materials from entrepreneurship forums, innovation summits, and policy workshops focused on food security. The goal is to trace how core interface concepts—such as 'surplus redistribution,' 'circular economy,' 'logistical platform,' and 'value recovery'—emerge, compete, and evolve within the discourse surrounding food excess and waste systems. By analyzing shifts in terminology, argumentative frameworks, and solution archetypes over time, the study will identify the conceptual bottlenecks and narrative drivers that shape the development of real-world secure food systems, offering a novel lens for understanding the innovation ecosystem itself.

ideas.title: Analyze role of informal institutions and culture
ideas.description: Integrate cultural dimensions or social norms into the model of VC and credit market effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Formal institutions, culture, and venture capital activity: A cross-country analysis

ideas.title: Triangulated Real-Time Capture of Entrepreneurial Cognitive Maps Under Induced Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research employs a controlled, longitudinal design to model the cognitive frameworks entrepreneurs use for marketing decisions under uncertainty, while directly mitigating reconstruction bias. Participants engage with a dynamic business simulation where market uncertainties are systematically introduced, requiring live marketing decisions. Their cognitive maps are captured concurrently via think-aloud protocols and psychophysiological measures during task performance, then later compared to retrospective map drawings and interview data. This triangulation allows for the construction of a robust model that distinguishes between the cognitive architecture used in the moment and the narratives built after the fact.

ideas.title: Apply ecological resilience models to regional SME clusters
ideas.description: Use frameworks from ecosystem science to model SME sector stability and recovery from Brexit shocks.
ideas.sourceTitle: International Business and Entrepreneurship Implications of Brexit

ideas.description: Investigate if impact on external stakeholders, not theory, should be the primary success metric.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Entrepreneurial Cognitive Landscapes
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in ecology—modeling a species' habitat niche—to the original idea of mapping entrepreneurial cognition. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) statistically map where a species can live based on environmental variables. We adapt this by treating an entrepreneur's past successful marketing/strategic decisions as species occurrences and the multidimensional conditions of market uncertainty (e.g., volatility, ambiguity, complexity) as the environmental layers. The output is a predictive, visual cognitive map modeling the 'decision niche'—the specific configurations of uncertainty within which the entrepreneur's cognitive framework is effectively deployed, allowing for analysis of framework robustness and transferability to new uncertain contexts.

ideas.title: Investigating Food Security through the Lens of Cognitive Resilience and System Antifragility
ideas.description: This research seed expands the conceptual exploration of food security by drawing an analogy from cognitive science and complex systems engineering, specifically the concepts of antifragility and cognitive resilience. The analogous problem—designing systems that benefit from shocks and uncertainty—has been addressed by mapping system responses to stressors and designing for optionality, modularity, and learning. Adapted to food security, this research would investigate a new dimension: the 'antifragility' of food systems. It would explore how food systems can be conceptually framed and practically engineered not merely to withstand or recover from disruptions (sustainability/resilience), but to use those disruptions as catalysts for improving access, nutritional quality, and equity. This involves studying stress-induced innovation pathways, the role of cognitive models in decision-making during crises, and the design of food networks with built-in adaptive redundancy, proposing a fundamental shift from defensive to proactive, learning-oriented food security paradigms.

ideas.title: Questioning the universality of food security coping strategies: An ecological resilience analogy
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that food security coping strategies for poverty and hunger are universally applicable by drawing an analogy from ecology and resilience theory. The analogous problem—the failure of universal models to predict ecosystem responses to disturbance—was solved by developing the panarchy framework, which maps system-specific variables, thresholds, and adaptive cycles across different regimes. The adapted idea applies this methodological shift to food security, proposing to map context-specific 'resilience landscapes' for households and communities. By identifying key interacting variables (like social networks, livelihood diversity, and governance structures) and their non-linear relationships across different socio-ecological contexts, the research would delineate why and how coping strategies are effective only within specific systemic regimes, thereby replacing universal models with a contingency-based understanding.

ideas.title: Multiplex Network Analysis of Power, Narrative, and Resource Flows in Food Security Governance
ideas.description: This research system employs a dynamic, multiplex network framework to analyze food security governance. It constructs and analyzes interconnected networks of formal collaborations, informal influence, shared policy narratives (extracted via NLP from documents and media), and resource dependencies (traced via budget allocations and funding flows). By applying temporal network analysis to this integrated model, the research identifies which actor coalitions successfully translate narrative influence into control over resources and policy decisions, revealing the hidden mechanics of power and policy stagnation or innovation in food systems.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Contact Tracing Networks to Map Influence Pathways in Food Security Governance
ideas.description: This research adapts the network analysis solution from epidemiological contact tracing to the context of food security governance. In epidemiology, contact tracing networks map pathogen transmission to identify super-spreaders and critical control points. The adapted idea applies this by treating seminal policy events or funding decisions as analogous to 'index cases' and tracing their influence pathways through the network of governance actors (e.g., ministries, UN agencies, agribusinesses, NGOs). By analyzing the resulting influence network's structure—identifying central 'super-connector' organizations, isolated clusters, and bottlenecks—the research will reveal the functional architecture of food security governance and pinpoint the most effective nodes for injecting policy innovation or resources to accelerate systemic change.

ideas.title: Causal Modeling of Governance-Socioeconomic Feedback Loops in Food Security
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond descriptive integration to rigorously model and test the causal mechanisms linking political governance and socioeconomic drivers of food security. It focuses on specific, measurable institutional variables (like policy stability or corruption control) and their interaction with economic factors such as trade openness or agricultural R&D investment. Using panel data, natural experiments, and agent-based modeling, the project identifies which governance interventions most effectively amplify or dampen the impact of socioeconomic drivers. The goal is to produce a diagnostic framework that predicts how policy changes in one domain will cascade through the integrated system to affect food security resilience.

ideas.title: Developing a Composite, Multi-Scale Index for Food Security by Adapting Ecosystem Health Assessment Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's ecosystem health assessments—which use composite indices to integrate diverse, multi-scale indicators into a holistic score—to the problem of updating food security metrics. The analogous problem of assessing complex, multi-faceted systems (like ecosystems) was solved by developing frameworks that combine core dimensions (e.g., biodiversity, resilience) into a single, interpretable index with clear reference states. The adapted idea is to systematically develop a composite, multi-scale food security index. This would involve defining key dimensions (availability, access, utilization, stability, sustainability, agency), selecting and weighting core indicators for each, and creating standardized protocols for data aggregation and benchmarking. The outcome would be a next-generation assessment tool that provides a more holistic, actionable, and comparable measure of food security across different regions and over time, directly informing policy and intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Socio-Technical Integration for Food Systems: A Cyber-Physical Systems Approach
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that management and policy are separate from technical agricultural solutions by drawing an analogy from Industrial Engineering and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). In manufacturing, the CPS framework solved the analogous problem of siloed physical automation and control software by creating integrated systems where both are co-designed. The adapted research idea applies this to food security by treating the farm ecosystem as a 'socio-technical' cyber-physical system. It proposes developing integrated modeling and design tools that simultaneously optimize biophysical technical solutions (like drought-resistant crops or precision agriculture hardware) with the 'control parameters' of management practices, economic incentives, and governance policies. This forces a fundamental integration, challenging the notion that food security can be solved by technical innovation alone before considering the human and institutional systems required for its deployment and sustainability.

ideas.title: Applying Microeconomic Game Theory to Model Intra-Household Food Allocation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from microeconomics and behavioral game theory. The analogous problem—understanding how a household with multiple agents makes joint economic decisions—was solved by moving beyond aggregate household data to model internal bargaining using frameworks like the Nash bargaining solution, considering factors like individual preferences, power, and external options. Adapting this solution, the research shifts focus from national/global food security assessments to explicitly model intra-household dynamics. It proposes treating the household as a strategic unit where food allocation results from negotiation, power structures, and individual member characteristics. By applying a microeconomic game-theoretic lens, the research aims to predict vulnerable individuals within 'food-secure' households, identify key decision-making levers, and design more precisely targeted interventions that alter the internal bargaining equilibrium to improve nutritional outcomes for all members.

ideas.title: Modeling Narrative Contagion in Food Security Policy Using Epidemiological Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from epidemiology and network science to the original idea of scaling cultural factors to global narratives in food security. The analogous problem—modeling the contagion and spread of a phenomenon through a network—was solved using compartmental models (like SIR) and network analysis to trace transmission pathways and identify critical nodes. The adapted idea applies this by treating dominant cultural narratives about food security (e.g., 'localism vs. global trade', 'techno-optimism vs. agro-ecology') as contagious agents. It will model their propagation through the global media and policy network, identifying key narrative 'vectors' (influential outlets, institutions), 'super-spreader' events (like major UN reports or food price crises), and policy environments with narrative 'immunity' or resistance. The goal is to quantitatively map and predict how these narrative contagions shape perceptions and ultimately influence food security policies at national and international levels.

ideas.title: A Co-evolutionary Framework for Formal Contracts and Informal Norms in ECF Governance
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from evolutionary ecology to investigate how formal contracts and informal community norms complement or substitute in ECF governance. The analogous problem in ecology—understanding how structured mutualisms and generalized interactions co-evolve under environmental stress—is solved using co-evolutionary models and network analysis to predict stable equilibria. The adapted idea applies this framework, modeling formal and informal governance as interacting entities whose effectiveness co-evolves with project-specific 'environmental pressures.' By employing agent-based simulations and analyzing historical ECF project networks, the study aims to map the conditions (e.g., high uncertainty, strong social ties) that lead to symbiotic reinforcement, competitive displacement, or functional substitution between these governance modes, providing predictive insights for designing resilient ECF systems.

ideas.title: A Coupled Systems Approach to Food Security: Modeling Governance and Socioeconomic Drivers as a Complex Adaptive System
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that socioeconomic and political factors in food security can be studied in isolation. Drawing an analogy from climate science, where Earth System Models successfully integrate atmosphere, ocean, land, and ice components, we propose adapting this 'coupled systems' solution. The analogous solution involved moving from isolated component models to integrated frameworks that simulate feedback loops and emergent phenomena. Adapted to food security, this leads to the development of a formal 'Food System Model' that treats governance and socioeconomic drivers as intrinsically coupled subsystems, similar to components in an ESM. This model would explicitly code the interactions and feedbacks between political decisions, market dynamics, social equity, and agricultural production, allowing researchers to simulate how perturbations in one subsystem cascade through the entire system. The goal is to move beyond correlation studies to a mechanistic understanding of food security as a complex adaptive system, identifying non-linear outcomes and synergistic policy interventions that would be invisible under a separated analytical framework.

ideas.title: Household-Level Foresight: Modeling Intra-Household Dynamics Under Alternative Food Security Futures
ideas.description: This research direction applies strategic foresight and scenario planning methods to the scale of the household unit. It develops detailed, qualitative scenarios of plausible long-term futures (e.g., pervasive automation, shifting gender norms, new food technologies) and uses them to model and simulate their impacts on intra-household decision-making, resource allocation, and nutritional outcomes. The approach reveals how future systemic pressures could alter household power structures, coping strategies, and inequities, providing a dynamic, forward-looking complement to static household surveys and enabling policymakers to stress-test social protection programs against a range of future household realities.

ideas.title: Causal identification of formal-informal governance interplay through codification and quasi-experiment
ideas.description: This research first conducts in-depth fieldwork to explicitly document and codify the specific, operative informal norms within an ECF community, transforming them from a latent variable into an observable set of rules. It then identifies a natural experiment, such as the staggered adoption of a new formal smart contract feature across different communities or a regulatory change, to serve as an exogenous shock to the formal governance layer. Using a difference-in-differences or regression discontinuity design, the study rigorously tests how this shock causes differential behavioral and performance outcomes, specifically testing for moderation by the pre-measured strength and content of the codified informal norms. This approach allows for causal inference about substitution and complementarity, moving beyond mere correlation.

ideas.title: Robust Decision-Making and Policy Stress-Testing for Food Security
ideas.description: This research system combines participatory scenario development with quantitative modeling and multi-criteria decision analysis to move from exploring futures to identifying actionable strategies. It stress-tests existing and proposed food security policies against a diverse set of quantified, probabilistic scenarios to evaluate their failure modes and co-benefits. The output is a prioritized portfolio of near-term interventions, investments, and monitoring indicators explicitly chosen for their robustness across uncertain futures and their capacity to preserve future adaptive options, thereby creating a direct, defensible link between foresight and current budgetary and planning cycles.

ideas.title: Modeling and Stress-Testing Incentive Realignments to Reduce Food Surplus in Secure Supply Systems
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive study to actively diagnose and redesign the incentive structures that cause secure food systems to generate excess and waste. It combines stakeholder analysis and institutional mapping to identify misaligned incentives for actors across retail, logistics, and policy domains. Using these maps, it builds agent-based models to simulate how proposed interventions (e.g., tax shifts, liability waivers, dynamic pricing mandates) would alter system-level surplus outcomes. Finally, the research designs and facilitates table-top 'policy stress tests' with stakeholders to probe the real-world robustness and political feasibility of the most promising incentive realignments.

ideas.title: Adapt Climate Model Downscaling for Food System Scenario Planning
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the 'downscaling' solution from climate science to the context of food security foresight. In climate science, global climate model projections are statistically or dynamically downscaled to provide locally relevant climate risk assessments. The adapted idea proposes to develop a similar methodological framework for food systems. By taking broad global scenarios (like the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways), the research would create a process to downscale them into detailed, region-specific food security scenarios. This involves integrating local data on crop yields, trade dependencies, infrastructure, and policy landscapes. The outcome would be a set of high-resolution future scenarios that enable national or regional policymakers to stress-test specific food security policies against a range of plausible, contextualized futures, making long-term strategic planning more robust and geographically grounded.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Network Analysis to Model and Redirect Food System Surplus
ideas.description: This study investigates the inverse problem of managing food excess and waste within secure systems by drawing an analogy from systems biology. The analogous field is Systems Biology/Biochemistry, where the solution to metabolic waste involves modeling biochemical networks to identify regulatory nodes and alternative pathways that redirect excess metabolite flux. The adapted idea applies metabolic flux analysis (MFA) principles to food systems, modeling the network of food production, distribution, and consumption. The research will identify key leverage points (analogous to allosteric regulators or gene expression controls) such as policy incentives, logistics hubs, and market signals that can dynamically redirect surplus food 'flux' from waste to productive uses, scaling solutions for both abundance and scarcity within the same secure system framework.

ideas.title: Apply epidemiological network modeling to simulate food system resilience
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from epidemiology, where agent-based models simulate disease spread through contact networks, to the context of food systems. In epidemiology, models with agents in states like susceptible or infected, connected by heterogeneous contact patterns, are used to forecast outbreaks and test containment policies. The adapted research idea applies this framework to simulate the propagation of socioeconomic 'shocks' (e.g., price spikes, trade disruptions) through a food system. Agents representing actors in the food chain (producers to consumers) are connected by their transactional and informational links, forming a network. By modeling shock propagation akin to disease transmission, the approach can test and optimize pre-emptive intervention strategies—such as creating redundant supply links or stabilizing information flows—to enhance the resilience and stability of the entire food system.

ideas.title: Develop predictive, high-frequency models for food security risk forecasting and early warning
ideas.description: This research moves beyond reviewing static indicators to construct dynamic, predictive models of food security risk. It integrates real-time and forward-looking data—such as climate forecasts, crop condition monitoring, commodity price volatility, and socio-economic telemetry—into a machine learning framework. The system is designed to produce actionable, sub-national risk forecasts and early-warning alerts, enabling proactive policy and humanitarian interventions before crises fully manifest. The outcome is a shift from measuring food insecurity to anticipating and mitigating it.

ideas.title: Dynamic Forecasting of Emerging Food Insecurity Risk Under Compound Stressors
ideas.description: This research develops a predictive modeling framework to forecast where and when populations will transition into high-risk food insecurity categories, rather than only identifying currently vulnerable groups. It focuses on modeling the complex interactions and feedback loops between climatic extremes, economic volatility, political instability, and global market shocks. The system produces probabilistic risk maps and scenario analyses to highlight emerging hotspots, enabling proactive, targeted interventions before crises fully manifest. The goal is to move policy and aid from reactive response to anticipatory action based on dynamic risk trajectories.
